parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker controls `dir` -> f-string builds SQL with `dir` -> `db.fetch_all(q)` executes the injected statement.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may execute arbitrary SQL (e.g., data exfiltration or modification) depending on the DB driver/permissions.
parsed_findings.proof: With `dir=asc;--`, the query becomes `SELECT id,email FROM users ORDER BY name asc;--`, so injected SQL is executed.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a request like `GET /users?sort=name&dir=asc;--` and observe DB errors or returned data changes.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `dir` from request is not validated and is interpolated into the SQL `ORDER BY` clause, enabling SQL injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Craft SSRF URL to internal host -> server fetches content -> response body is returned to attacker.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can reach internal-only services and exfiltrate sensitive data through the proxy endpoint.
parsed_findings.proof: Request `/fetch?url=http://127.0.0.1:80/` (or `http://169.254.169.254/`) returns the internal response body via `res.send(data.data)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: 1) Start an internal endpoint with known output; 2) GET `/fetch?url=http://<internal-host>:<port>/`; 3) Read leaked content in response.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `req.query.url` is fetched by the server via `axios.get(u)` with no validation/allowlist, enabling SSRF.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Control fetched response body -> browser interprets as HTML -> JS executes in victim context.
parsed_findings.impact: Reflected client-side script execution can lead to session theft, phishing, and actions as the victim user.
parsed_findings.proof: Host HTML at an attacker URL that returns `<script>alert(1)</script>`, then visit `/fetch?url=http://attacker/xss.html`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: 1) Serve attacker HTML/JS from `http://attacker/xss.html`; 2) Victim loads `/fetch?url=http://attacker/xss.html`; 3) Script runs if browser treats it as HTML.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The server proxies fetched content verbatim with `res.send(data.data)`, so an attacker-controlled URL can return executable HTML/JS to the client.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Stored XSS (requires HTML rendering) -> run attacker JS -> steal tokens/CSRF as victims view notes.
parsed_findings.impact: If notes are rendered as HTML, this enables stored XSS and potential account/session compromise.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 6 assigns inv.note = request.json.get('note', inv.note) and line 7 calls db.save(inv) with no filtering.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: As an authorized editor, PATCH /api/invoices/<inv_id> with {"note":"<script>alert(1)</script>"}, then view the invoice note in the UI.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: update_invoice writes user-controlled request.json.note directly into inv.note and persists it without validation/sanitization.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Invalid inv_id -> inv is null -> server exception -> potential info leak/DoS.
parsed_findings.impact: Enables DoS and may leak error details/stack traces depending on server configuration.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 3 assigns inv = db.get_invoice(inv_id) and line 4 immediately reads inv.project_id in user_can_edit().
parsed_findings.repro_steps: PATCH /api/invoices/<nonexistent_id> as any user; observe a 500/exception from accessing inv.project_id.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Missing null-check for inv can cause a server error when inv_id does not map to an invoice.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker controls `req.query.file` -> weak `../` stripping -> `path.join('/srv/data',f)` escapes base -> `fs.readFile` discloses contents.
parsed_findings.impact: Arbitrary file disclosure from the server filesystem via path traversal.
parsed_findings.proof: Request `/download?file=..%2f..%2f..%2fetc%2fpasswd` makes `p` point to `/etc/passwd`, then `fs.readFile(p,'utf8')` returns it.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: GET `/download?file=..%2f..%2f..%2fetc%2fpasswd` and observe the response body contains `/etc/passwd`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The `file` query is sanitized with a single `replace('../','')`, allowing traversal payloads that resolve outside `/srv/data`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker supplies absolute `file` -> `path.join` resolves outside `/srv/data` -> `fs.readFile` reads the target file -> response exfiltrates it.
parsed_findings.impact: Direct arbitrary file read if absolute paths are permitted by the runtime path resolution.
parsed_findings.proof: Request `/download?file=/etc/passwd` causes `p` to resolve to `/etc/passwd`, which is then read and sent back.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: GET `/download?file=/etc/passwd` and verify the response includes sensitive system file contents.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: An absolute-path `file` value can bypass the base directory because `path.join('/srv/data', f)` can resolve to the attacker-supplied absolute path.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker crafts malicious pickle -> base64-encodes and sets `state` cookie -> server decodes and calls `pickle.loads` -> gadget chain executes -> attacker gains code execution.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can execute code on the server with the web app’s process privileges, leading to full compromise.
parsed_findings.proof: `raw` comes from `request.cookies.get('state')`, then `pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(raw))` runs attacker-supplied bytes as Python objects.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Create a malicious pickle payload (with a known `__reduce__` gadget), base64-encode it, set it as the `state` cookie, then request `GET /resume`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The handler deserializes user-controlled cookie data with `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary code execution via malicious pickle gadgets.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use mass-assignment to set privileged fields, then perform unauthorized actions gated by those fields.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can escalate privileges or modify account attributes they should not be able to change.
parsed_findings.proof: users.js line 6 assigns `req.body` onto `user` via `Object.assign(user, req.body)` before `await db.users.save(user)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `PATCH /api/users/{id}` with JSON like `{"isSupport":true,"role":"admin"}` to set privileged attributes.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Mass-assignment lets clients overwrite sensitive user fields because `Object.assign(user, req.body)` applies all request properties to the model.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Exploit authorization weakness to target another user, then use mass-assignment to modify sensitive fields.
parsed_findings.impact: Unauthorized modification of arbitrary users’ records (broken access control/IDOR).
parsed_findings.proof: users.js line 2 checks only `req.user.id`/`req.user.isSupport` and the logic shown does not reference `req.params.id` before fetching the target with `db.users.findById(req.params.id)` on line 5.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: As a non-support user, authenticate and try `PATCH /api/users/{otherUserId}`; if line-2 logic effectively bypasses, the request updates the other user on line 6-7.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The authorization check appears detached from `req.params.id`, enabling potential IDOR where users can patch other users if the condition is incorrect or always passes.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: User controls `name`/`src` -> command string interpolation -> `os.system` shell execution -> arbitrary commands run.
parsed_findings.impact: Arbitrary command execution lets an attacker read/modify data and potentially gain a shell.
parsed_findings.proof: `cmd = f'tar -czf /tmp/{name}.tgz {src}'` followed by `os.system(cmd)` runs through the shell.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/backup` with `{"src":"/var/data/app;id #","name":"daily"}` to execute `id`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted `name`/`src` are interpolated into a shell command and executed via `os.system`, enabling OS command injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: User controls `name` -> `/tmp/{name}.tgz` path is constructed -> `tar` writes to traversed path.
parsed_findings.impact: May allow file creation/overwrite outside the intended temp directory depending on server permissions.
parsed_findings.proof: No normalization/validation is applied to `name` before it is embedded into the output path.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/backup` with `{"name":"../pwn","src":"/var/data/app"}` to attempt writing outside `/tmp` as `/pwn.tgz`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `name` controls the output filename `/tmp/{name}.tgz`, allowing path traversal and unintended file writes.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Inject HTML via markdown input 2) Persist as html 3) Client executes when displaying that html.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker can run arbitrary JavaScript in victims’ browsers, enabling session theft and UI redressing.
parsed_findings.proof: POST /comments with body "<script>alert(1)</script>"; markdown() output is stored as db.insert_comment({'html': rendered}).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: 1) POST /comments {"body":"<script>alert(1)</script>"} 2) GET /comments 3) Render items[].html in a page without escaping.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unsanitized markdown is converted to HTML and stored/served, enabling stored XSS if the client renders the returned html directly.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send a negative amount to reverse the transfer direction and choose any `to` account to debit.
parsed_findings.impact: Unauthorized balance manipulation and potential fund theft or account sabotage via negative transfers.
parsed_findings.proof: `amt=int(request.json['amount'])` and the `src < amt` check do not prevent `amt<0`, so `db.set_balance(current_user.id, src-amt)` credits the attacker.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /transfer with JSON like `{"to":"victim_id","amount":"-100"}` while having `src` >= 0.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The endpoint allows negative `amount`, letting an attacker increase their own balance while decreasing the target balance.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Choose a victim `to` id and a valid `amount` to drain from `current_user.id` into the victim account.
parsed_findings.impact: IDOR-style unauthorized fund transfers between arbitrary accounts.
parsed_findings.proof: `to=request.json['to']` is used directly in `db.set_balance(to, db.get_balance(to)+amt)` without verifying the requester is allowed to credit that account.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Identify a victim account id and POST /transfer with JSON `{"to":"victim_id","amount":"50"}`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `to` is user-controlled and the code transfers funds to any account id without authorization checks.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Forge JWT with attacker-chosen claims -> ensure `jwt.verify` throws (bad signature) -> in production hit `jwt.decode` fallback -> downstream authorization trusts returned claims.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can authenticate/obtain authorization based on claims in forged JWTs, causing account takeover and privilege escalation.
parsed_findings.proof: The catch block returns `jwt.decode(t)` when `process.env.NODE_ENV == 'production'`, so tokens that fail signature verification still yield a payload.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send an `Authorization: Bearer <forged JWT>` with an invalid RS256 signature; `jwt.verify` throws and `verifyToken` returns decoded claims in production.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: In production, failed `jwt.verify` falls back to `jwt.decode`, returning unverified token claims and enabling signature forgery-based auth bypass.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Single-step IDOR: authenticated request with different `id` values to retrieve other users' records.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can enumerate or access arbitrary users' sensitive data (emails) by changing the `id` parameter.
parsed_findings.proof: The endpoint selects `email` from `users` by the provided `uid` and returns `row` directly to the requester.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: As any authenticated user, call `GET /u?id=1` and observe the JSON response containing `email` for user id 1.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `/u` uses a caller-supplied `id` to fetch and return another user's email without any object-level authorization check.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the timing oracle to recover a valid signature, then proceed to send malicious payloads.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker could forge valid `X-Sig` values and submit unauthorized webhook events.
parsed_findings.proof: The code does `if got == want:` rather than using a constant-time comparison like `hmac.compare_digest`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Repeatedly POST to `/hook` with identical body while varying `X-Sig`, then measure response timing to infer correct HMAC bytes.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The HMAC signature comparison uses non-constant-time equality, enabling a timing side-channel to distinguish partial matches.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: First obtain a valid `X-Sig` (e.g., via the timing side-channel), then send oversized JSON to trigger DoS in `json.loads`/`process_event`.
parsed_findings.impact: Denial of service or application instability via expensive JSON parsing and event processing.
parsed_findings.proof: On a matched signature, it executes `process_event(json.loads(body))` where `body` comes directly from the request.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: With a valid `X-Sig`, POST an oversized or deeply nested JSON body to `/hook` and observe high CPU/memory usage or 500 errors.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The handler parses attacker-controlled JSON without payload size limits or error handling, risking resource exhaustion.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: User sets `next` to `//evil.com` -> `redirect(nxt)` uses it -> browser follows redirect off-site.
parsed_findings.impact: Open redirect enables phishing and credential/token theft via off-site navigation and trust abuse.
parsed_findings.proof: GET /go?next=//attacker.com will pass the `startswith('/')` check and be used directly as the redirect target.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: 1) Send request to `/go?next=//attacker.com` 2) Observe HTTP 302 Location pointing to `//attacker.com`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The handler redirects to user-controlled `next` when it only checks `startswith('/')`, allowing scheme-relative URLs like `//attacker.com` to bypass origin restrictions.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-601","file":"redirect.py","line_start":3,"line_end":6,"vuln_claim":"The handler redirects to user-controlled `next` when it only checks `startswith('/')`, allowing scheme-relative URLs like `//attacker.com` to bypass origin restrictions.","proof":"GET /go?next=//attacker.com will pass the `startswith('/')` check and be used directly as the redirect target.","repro_steps":"1) Send request to `/go?next=//attacker.com` 2) Observe HTTP 302 Location pointing to `//attacker.com`.","impact":"Open redirect enables phishing and credential/token theft via off-site navigation and trust abuse.","confidence":0.65,"chain_steps":"User sets `next` to `//evil.com` -> `redirect(nxt)` uses it -> browser follows redirect off-site."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Craft XML with external entity, let the parser resolve it during `fromstring`, then use `findtext('title')` to return the resolved content.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may read local files or reach internal endpoints via SSRF and exfiltrate data through the returned `title`.
parsed_findings.proof: `resolve_entities=True` with `no_network=False` allows external entity expansion during `etree.fromstring`, and response includes extracted `title` content.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/xml/import` with a DTD defining an external entity (e.g., `<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">`) and set `<title>&xxe;</title>`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The XML parser enables entity resolution and network access, so attacker-controlled XML can trigger XXE via external entities.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send recursive entity payload, trigger expansion in `etree.fromstring` due to entity resolution, and exhaust resources before the handler returns.
parsed_findings.impact: The service can be slowed or crashed due to excessive entity expansion during XML parsing.
parsed_findings.proof: `resolve_entities=True` permits expanded entities during parsing, and there is no shown protection like secure processing limits.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/xml/import` with a DTD that defines recursively expanding entities (billion laughs) and observe CPU/memory exhaustion or timeout.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Entity expansion is not constrained, enabling DoS from large/billion-laughs style recursive entities when parsing attacker XML.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Craft operator-style fields in the request body to alter `q`, match a user record, and reach any success branch.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may bypass authentication or cause account compromise via query manipulation.
parsed_findings.proof: `q` is built from `req.body.email`/`req.body.password` and passed as-is to `db.users.findOne(q)` (lines 2-3).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/login` with JSON like `{"email":{"$ne":null},"password":{"$ne":null}}` and observe authentication succeed.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The login query uses raw `req.body` values directly in `db.users.findOne(q)`, enabling NoSQL/operator injection if the database interprets objects.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Submit any credentials to trigger the always-true branch and inherit whatever response/session creation the route performs.
parsed_findings.impact: Unauthenticated users may obtain a valid session/token if the success path is unconditional.
parsed_findings.proof: The snippet contains `if ( true });` on line 4, rather than validating `user` and the provided password.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send any POST to `/login`; if the success logic is inside the `if`, it will always execute.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The credential check appears replaced by an unconditional `if ( true )`, which can result in authentication being granted regardless of input.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Provide internal `url`; 2) handler fetches with `requests.get`; 3) response reveals fetched content size.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker can probe internal services (and metadata) and exfiltrate info via observable responses (size).
parsed_findings.proof: POST `{"url":"http://127.0.0.1:8000/"}` makes the server fetch that internal resource and report its content size.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send POST to `/avatar/fetch` with JSON `{ "url": "http://127.0.0.1:8000/" }` and observe response `{ "size": ... }`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unrestricted `requests.get(url)` enables SSRF to internal or cloud metadata endpoints via attacker-supplied `url`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Point `url` to a large payload; 2) server loads full body into RAM; 3) memory/worker exhaustion causes outage.
parsed_findings.impact: Resource exhaustion can degrade or crash the service and deny availability to other users.
parsed_findings.proof: Using a URL that streams a very large body (or slow chunking) can exhaust memory/worker resources.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/avatar/fetch` with a URL serving a large response (e.g., huge file) and monitor CPU/memory/worker availability.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Downloads full remote content into memory (`.content`) without size limits, enabling DoS by large responses.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Stack overflow can corrupt return address to redirect execution into attacker-controlled data or gadgets.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker-controlled overwrite can lead to denial of service and potentially arbitrary code execution.
parsed_findings.proof: Supplying more than 63 bytes overwrites adjacent stack data including saved control-flow values.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Compile and run, then input a long string (e.g., 100 'A's) at the prompt; observe crash or abnormal behavior.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer, enabling stack buffer overflow.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Extract SharedPreferences -> obtain `access_token` -> replay token to auth endpoints -> impersonate the victim user.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can exfiltrate the bearer token and hijack the user's session until the token expires or is revoked.
parsed_findings.proof: `saveToken()` calls `ctx.getSharedPreferences('auth',MODE_PRIVATE)` then `p.edit().putString('access_token',token).apply()` with no cryptographic protection.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: On a rooted/emulator device or via adb backup, locate SharedPreferences `auth` and read `access_token` from the stored XML.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The code stores an access token in Android SharedPreferences without encryption, enabling credential/session theft from the app's storage.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-312","file":"SessionStore.kt","line_start":3,"line_end":5,"vuln_claim":"The code stores an access token in Android SharedPreferences without encryption, enabling credential/session theft from the app's storage.","proof":"`saveToken()` calls `ctx.getSharedPreferences('auth',MODE_PRIVATE)` then `p.edit().putString('access_token',token).apply()` with no cryptographic protection.","repro_steps":"On a rooted/emulator device or via adb backup, locate SharedPreferences `auth` and read `access_token` from the stored XML.","impact":"An attacker can exfiltrate the bearer token and hijack the user's session until the token expires or is revoked.","confidence":0.7,"chain_steps":"Extract SharedPreferences -> obtain `access_token` -> replay token to auth endpoints -> impersonate the victim user."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the first 16 bytes as IV and decrypt the remaining ciphertext blocks with the known AES key.
parsed_findings.impact: Loss of confidentiality for all encrypted data.
parsed_findings.proof: KEY is directly set to `b'0123456789abcdef'` and used for AES-CBC encryption.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Obtain the source (or key) and decrypt `iv+ciphertext` with AES-CBC using the known KEY and the prepended IV.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The AES key is hard-coded as a constant byte string, so anyone with code/ciphertexts can decrypt data.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Flip bits in the IV to alter the first plaintext block after decryption, then iterate to tamper with subsequent blocks.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker can tamper with plaintext and potentially recover data if error behavior is observable.
parsed_findings.proof: The function returns only `iv + cipher.encrypt(...)` with no MAC/tag.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Modify ciphertext (or IV) bits and feed the altered blob to the corresponding decrypt routine to induce controlled plaintext changes.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: AES-CBC is used without any authentication (e.g., HMAC/GCM), enabling ciphertext malleability and potential integrity/padding-oracle issues downstream.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Provide `out=NULL` with `out_sz>0` -> guard condition evaluates false -> compute `n` -> `memcpy`/write to `out[n]` dereferences NULL.
parsed_findings.impact: NULL pointer dereference causing a denial of service (process crash).
parsed_findings.proof: Calling `copy_name("AAAA",NULL,5)` makes the guard bypass (out is NULL, out_sz!=0) and then executes `memcpy(out, in, n)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: 1) Pass `out=NULL` and `out_sz>0` to `copy_name`. 2) Observe a crash on `memcpy`/`out[n]='\0'`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The guard `if (out || out_sz == 0) return -1;` fails to reject `out == NULL`, so `memcpy(out,...)` and `out[n]='\0'` can dereference a NULL pointer.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-476","file":"safe_copy.c","line_start":3,"line_end":6,"vuln_claim":"The guard `if (out || out_sz == 0) return -1;` fails to reject `out == NULL`, so `memcpy(out,...)` and `out[n]='\\0'` can dereference a NULL pointer.","proof":"Calling `copy_name(\"AAAA\",NULL,5)` makes the guard bypass (out is NULL, out_sz!=0) and then executes `memcpy(out, in, n)`.","repro_steps":"1) Pass `out=NULL` and `out_sz>0` to `copy_name`. 2) Observe a crash on `memcpy`/`out[n]='\\0'`.","impact":"NULL pointer dereference causing a denial of service (process crash).","confidence":0.8,"chain_steps":"Provide `out=NULL` with `out_sz>0` -> guard condition evaluates false -> compute `n` -> `memcpy`/write to `out[n]` dereferences NULL."}]}
